text: The reason why earthing does not make a difference to rectified antenna energy is that some antennas dont need an earth. Check RFID tags they usually use a symmetric dipole which is matched to the load. So to transfer all the energy, the antenna has to be designed to match the load impedance eg the joule thief.This could be slight hard but not impossible and can make a huge difference..

author: @RWBHere
text:
It's effectively a crude crystal set, charging a capacitor, in that last experiment. Erect an appropriate aerial near to a decent radio station or near to power lines (safely) and you'll be able to light fluorescent tubes. When you mentioned adding a Ground lead having little or no effect, that was likely because the circuit is effectively grounded via the multimeter, either being Earthed, or if battery-powered, by it acting as a counterpoise to the graphene sheet which is acting like a capacitive 'top hat' to the short aerial lead. A sheet of Aluminium foil would have worked better. You also need to consider the microamps of electrical leakage through, and the possible milliamps of RF coupling around, the unscreened multimeter (and other) leads. That alone could charge the small capacitor in that 'detector' circuit.

Off-topic, for a moment: Have you considered making a graphene or graphite micro-structure on a substrate, such that it consists of what are effectively randomly oriented and sized dipoles or monopoles, and harvesting electricity from incident Infra red light sources, including sunlight, waste heat, etc? Something like it has been done before, but not using graphene or graphite, but it's food for thought, perhaps?

As for the first demonstration, I'm afraid that the tests were quite meaningless. You need to use an RF quiet chamber, giving you at least 100 dB of attenuation of external RF interference. (A 10 kW RF source outside is essentially undetectable inside the chamber.) Those are very expensive. You also need a standard RF source, a proper isolation jig (lots more interference rejection), plus some sensitive test gear. DC lighting is used. Even someone moving around inside the chamber will affect results, if the test jig is not isolating the test device from their body's electrical disturbances. I was fortunate enough to have used such a chamber professionally, for low-power tests between about 100 MHz and 15 GHz. It's not a casual process, and careful methodology is essential if you want useful data. Two people moving around inside the shielded and grounded metal-clad chamber would be a complete waste of time! As would somebody opening the door slightly at a critical moment. You put a 'Keep Out!' notice up, and work alone. We even had to leave our digital watches outside the test chamber, because of the RF noise they generate. Interesting work though.

Rather unfortunately, what you have shown is that a simple and cheap sheet of Aluminium foil is at least as effective as an expensive Carbon-covered sheet of substrate. Apologies if this shoots you down in flames; it's not meant to be negative, but rather to point out that your method is incorrect, and thus meaningless. 

Thanks for reading this.

text: I have been experimenting for years trying to sus out a radiant receiving system with useful.  The issue I have hit is the inability to place multiple receivers into parallel or series diode banks with a single output. As a test bank I have placed ten aluminium foil receivers into a bank running into separate germanium diode banks to a single cap. There was no increase in useful. Perhaps there is a shortfall within circuit layout which you could illustrate within your experiment. Tuning is currently designed about am radio frequencies.

Use a bridge rectifier per antenna, consider using a tuning circuit as well, the peek to peek signal on a tuned frequency is much more regular than an untuned frequency. Without tuning you are just rectifying static, with tuning you have an actual signal to work with.

still though 1 antenna to 1 tuner to 1 bridge rectifier and you can collect a few milliiwatts if you're lucky

author: @nigeljohnson9820
text:
From the RF point of view there are so many things wrong with these experiments, particularly the last one. The best that can be said is that the provide an indication of the performance of these coatings as RF screening.
One question worth asking; what unique property do these coatings offer over say conventional metal/metal conductive coatings?
I am not trying to make negative comments, I am suggesting that the material needs to be studied to identify unique properties that will increase its value or find applications where its properties are superior.

replies.author: @seanbandes648

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820

replies.author: @stevenbishop8625
replies.text: calm down he's not fillng a patent application

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text: @stevenbishop8625 having read a large number of patent applications and generated a few. I can assure you that a workable design is not a required to get a patent granted. If you cannot identify the flaws in the methodology being used in these tests you clearly do not have much understanding of RF. As I said, these crude tests can only provide a vague indication of screening effectiveness, they certainly cannot provide any quantitative information. It is also doubtful that the comparison can be relied on. There is so much wrong with the test methodology it is difficult to know where to start. A WiFi modem is really not the best RF source, given its mode of operation and I am not sure what is to be gained by wrapping the material around a concrete column and connecting to a scavenger circuit via a long wire. I doubt it  will tell you much about the material. The connecting wire is likely to dominate any RF pickup. Certainly it is very unlikely that much of the WiFi signal will be captured by the arrangement. It's more likely to capture VHF radio energy or 50Hz mains pick-up. RF testing in the open is notoriously difficult, even with sophisticated test equipment. Field strength tests are usually conducted in an RF anechoic chamber.

replies.author: @nigeljohnson9820
replies.text:
I think it is unfair to expect someone who does not appear to have a background in RF engineering to conduct relatively difficult RF measurements with  simple test equipment. A minimum setup might be a calibrated RF signal generator, an RF spectrum analyser and a set of matched aerials. In order to minimise RF reflections from the environment, such test can be conducted on an open air test site, away from sources of reflection. In the lab, at frequencies above 1GHz, it might be possible to use a large metal box, with a receiver aerial inside, with the open end fitted with window of the material under test. There are many other less direct measurements that could be done, such as using the material as a printed transmission line and measuring its matching parameters. There is a long history of coating the internal surfaces of mobile phone cases with EMC absorbing materials, from memory, One such coating was known as aquardag. Over the years, the need for such coatings have reduced as matched impedance tracking and multilayer circuit boards have greatly reduced the radiated emissions for such boards. One only needs to look at PC desk top cases fitted with transparent panels, to know that RFI issues are dealt with at the PCB level.
I would be interested to know if the copper plating of the conductive graphene coating offers any advantage of the traditional method of making flexible copper on plastic components and interconnects. I suspect it depends on being able to remove the graphene material after the copper has been mask coated onto the graphene coated plastic. There are inkjet printers that can print PCB track patterns using conductive ink.

replies.author: @ericlinz4626
replies.text:
@marthale7   There is some kind of relationship between the wavelength and the ability to penetrate or to see.

I "think" (from my limited research) that the shorter wavelengths (like 1 millimeter) tend to react more with the atmosphere and generally scatter more easily.

The longer wavelengths actually pass through objects more easily but give less feedback because there is less scatter.

So if you want *both* penetration and scatter you would use a millimeter length wave and higher power.

Which ironically is what 5G and the military weapon that makes you jump off buildings (feels like fire) is all about.

The weapon is orders of magnitude more powerful though.
